---
title: "Working Memory Demo"
subtitle: "Digit span and chunking"
---

This quick activity demonstrates that working memory is limited and that organization changes what the mind can hold.

::: {.callout-note title="Connection to the chapter"}
Use this demo with **Section 1: Attention Is the Gateway to Learning**. Students should experience the working-memory limit first, then connect the experience to chunking, cognitive load, and why organized studying works better than passive rereading.
:::

<iframe src="interactives/digit-span.html" title="Working Memory Digit Span Demo" width="100%" height="820" style="border: 1px solid #d8e0e8; border-radius: 14px;"></iframe>

## Debrief Questions

1. At what digit length did the task become difficult?
2. Did you feel yourself trying to group the digits into chunks?
3. Why is the chunked example easier than the unchunked example?
4. How does this explain why disconnected facts are hard to study?
5. How could you use chunking when studying psychology terms?

::: {.callout-important title="Key Takeaway"}
Working memory does not simply hold a fixed amount of information. It holds information better when that information is organized into meaningful chunks.
:::
